html:
Epilogue

I

Teresa Boselli stood and faced Maddalena with the parlor table between them; on the table was lying that short official letter‚ÅÝ‚Äîthe cold, white messenger of death.

‚ÄúI have come,‚Äù said Maddalena, ‚Äúbecause it was my duty, and because Gian-Luca would have wished it.‚Äù

‚ÄúYou have come,‚Äù said Teresa, ‚Äúbecause he was my grandson, the child of my only child.‚Äù

Maddalena‚Äôs face was as white as the paper, and her mothering eyes were tearless. ‚ÄúTomorrow I go to Lyndhurst,‚Äù she answered; ‚ÄúFather Antonio has promised to go with me, and Rosa, who loved her foster-son, and Mario and old Nerone.‚Äù Her voice was quite steady but curiously toneless, as though a dead creature were speaking.

‚ÄúI also will accompany you,‚Äù said Teresa.

‚ÄúWhy?‚Äù inquired Maddalena.

Teresa Boselli drew herself up, and her hard, black eyes were defiant. ‚ÄúWhy?‚Äù she said harshly; ‚Äúbecause my blood calls. Was he not the child of my child?‚Äù

Then Maddalena‚Äôs eyes grew as hard as Teresa‚Äôs, and her gentle face stiffened to anger. ‚ÄúToo late you remember,‚Äù she answered coldly, ‚Äúyou who would never love Gian-Luca.‚Äù

After that they were silent, staring at each other, while their bitterness leapt out between them; the little back parlor was trembling with it, with the dull, heavy thuds of their hearts. Maddalena quietly picked up the letter, which she thrust into the bosom of her dress, then she turned and went out of the Casa Boselli‚ÅÝ‚Äîleaving Teresa alone.

Six black-clad figures got down from the train when it stopped at Lyndhurst station.

‚ÄúThis must be the lot that have come for the inquest,‚Äù whispered a porter to his friend.

Very calm and noble looked Maddalena, clothed in the weeds of her affliction; she walked straight forward into her grief, like the brave Roman matron that she was. Her eyes held no hope and no resentment, they were filled with the finality of fate; and after her followed Teresa Boselli, scarcely less steadfast and upright.

II

Rosa was clinging to Mario‚Äôs arm, and Rosa must openly weep: ‚ÄúMio bambino‚ÅÝ‚Äîmio cuore‚ÅÝ‚Äî‚Äù she wept.

And Mario muttered: ‚ÄúHe did all, all for me‚ÅÝ‚Äîeven in his trouble he remembered poor Mario and got him a post at the Doric.‚Äù

Nerone stumped beside them with his chin sunk on his breast; he was swinging his wooden leg slowly, for his loins were no longer so strong as they had been and the wooden leg felt very heavy. And ever at the heels of this sad little flock there hovered a faithful old sheepdog‚ÅÝ‚ÄîFather Antonio, whose hair had turned silver, and whose eyes were the cloudy blue of an infant‚Äôs, as is sometimes the way with the eyes of a man whose life‚Äôs circle is nearing completion. He it was who drove with Maddalena in the fly, and who quietly held her hand.

‚ÄúSo God has taken Gian-Luca‚ÅÝ‚Äî‚Äù he said thoughtfully, and he added: ‚ÄúGod surely knows best.‚Äù

But Maddalena answered: ‚ÄúThere is doubt in my heart‚ÅÝ‚ÄîI am doubting the mercy of God.‚Äù Then she put her doubts and her fears into words, while Father Antonio listened.

What would happen to the poor lonely soul of Gian-Luca? He had died all unshriven by Holy Church‚ÅÝ‚Äîall alone he had died, without priest or candle, and no one had been near to pray for his soul that it might have a merciful passing. And as she went on speaking her voice shook a little with the superstitious dread of the peasant.

‚ÄúTo die like that, on the cold earth‚ÅÝ‚Äî‚Äù she whispered; ‚Äúand he never received the precious body of Our Lord‚ÅÝ‚Äîthere was no one to put the blessed oil on his eyelids, or on his tired, travel-stained feet‚ÅÝ‚Äî‚Äù

Then Father Antonio looked at her gravely: ‚ÄúThe earth has been blest for always, my daughter‚ÅÝ‚ÄîGod walked upon the earth in the Garden of Eden, and again on His way to Calvary. The Church is a Mother whose arms are wide open to succor and comfort her children, but God is a Father whose heart is eternal, and many a sheep that has never been penned will be gathered to the fold of that heart.‚Äù He gazed at the splendor of the woods that they were passing, all flaming they were with gold and crimson. ‚ÄúEngland is a beautiful church,‚Äù he murmured. He often said queer things like that these days, they thought it was because he was so old.

III

Gian-Luca‚Äôs body was lying in a stable attached to an unimportant inn. Some new red bricks were piled in a corner, together with a number of workmen‚Äôs tools‚ÅÝ‚Äîthey were going to turn the place into a garage, and the work had been hastily suspended. A silent police sergeant unlocked the door and allowed the mourners to enter; then he quietly turned back the sheet from the body, and standing aside, he waited.

‚ÄúSanta Madonna!‚Äù muttered Nerone, ‚Äúthis is an outrage, a scandal! No chapel, no reverence, not even for the dead; what a pagan country is England!‚Äù

But Gian-Luca lay very much at rest in his stable, with his thin hands folded on his breast; and seeing him thus, Nerone fell silent. Finding his rosary, he began to tell his beads, marking the prayers with his thumb. Then they all gathered round, those members of the clan, to say goodbye to their child.

‚ÄúHe was born in our midst, the Gian-Luca‚ÅÝ‚Äî‚Äù sobbed Rosa.

‚ÄúDavvero,‚Äù murmured Mario, ‚Äúhe was born in our midst.‚Äù And remembering the past as folk will at such times, he also could not check his tears.

Teresa went close and stared down at her grandson, and none could know what she was thinking, for her face was the face of an ivory idol in which glowed two points of flame.

‚ÄúWhat do they say that he died of?‚Äù she demanded, walking over to the sergeant.

The sergeant looked embarrassed and glanced at Maddalena. ‚ÄúWell, of course, there‚Äôll have to be an inquest,‚Äù he murmured, ‚Äúbut I‚Äôve heard that they think he died of starvation and exposure‚ÅÝ‚Äîit‚Äôs been chilly these nights.‚Äù Then, because Teresa still fixed him with those eyes, the sergeant must go blundering on: ‚ÄúIt‚Äôs peculiar, though, we found money on the body‚ÅÝ‚Äîover two pounds‚ÅÝ‚Äîbut it‚Äôs not for me to talk,‚Äù he concluded hastily.

Teresa Boselli went back to the coffin and looked down at the wasted face. ‚ÄúSo much food in the world,‚Äù she muttered, ‚Äúand they think that he died of starvation!‚Äù

Maddalena bent forward and kissed Gian-Luca, very gently she kissed his forehead. ‚ÄúHe looks so strange,‚Äù she whispered to her priest, ‚Äúas though he were not here at all‚ÅÝ‚Äî‚Äù

‚ÄúWhy should Gian-Luca be here?‚Äù he answered. ‚ÄúDid Our Lord remain in His tomb?‚Äù

And now Father Antonio took something from his neck, a little crucifix in gold, and he placed it gently on Gian-Luca‚Äôs hands. ‚ÄúMay this Man who died and this God who lives, keep your soul unto life everlasting,‚Äù he said slowly, then he dropped on his knees by the side of the coffin and began to read the prayers for the dead.

Mario knelt down, and Rosa and Nerone, joining in those charitable prayers; but grief suddenly caught Maddalena by the throat, so that she could not pray, and it scourged her and shook her like a reed in the storm‚ÅÝ‚Äî

‚ÄúHe starved, he was homeless and cold and forsaken!‚Äù she cried out, wringing her hands.

Then Maddalena wept for her unborn children and for the father of her unborn children, himself so much her child. The merciful tears streamed down her cheeks unheeded, while the sharp plough of sorrow furrowed her soul; and her tears fell one by one into those furrows, nourishing the sacred seed of the spirit, the seed of faith in the mercy of God, that must come to fruition through tears.

Rosa touched Mario quietly on the shoulder, and together they left the stable. Then Nerone got awkwardly up from his knees, and he too left the stable, trying to walk softly, trying not to tap with his old wooden leg that made such a noise on the stones. But Teresa Boselli still lingered by the coffin, for she could not take her eyes from her grandson.

‚ÄúSo much food in the world,‚Äù she kept repeating, ‚Äúand they think that he died of starvation!‚Äù

When old Teresa had at last looked her fill, she walked away firmly with her back very upright; she scarcely appeared to require that stick, so strong, it seemed, were her knees.

Outside in the street she said to a passer: ‚ÄúWhere is our Catholic Church?‚Äù

‚ÄúThe Roman Catholic church is down there on the right-hand side,‚Äù she was told.

Teresa went into the quiet, empty church‚ÅÝ‚Äîshe had not entered a church until now for thirty-four angry years. Right up to the statue of the Virgin walked Teresa‚ÅÝ‚Äîthe statue that stood at the foot of the altar‚ÅÝ‚Äîand the Virgin‚Äôs halo was set with seven stars, perhaps for her seven sorrows.

‚ÄúSo,‚Äù said Teresa, confronting the Madonna, ‚Äúso this is what you have done! I gave you Gian-Luca, the little Gian-Luca‚ÅÝ‚Äîall helpless and cold and motherless he was‚ÅÝ‚Äîand I said to you: ‚ÄòTake him, I give him to you, you can have him body and soul!‚Äô And you took him, and you broke him, and you starved him to death out there in the cold, English forest‚ÅÝ‚Äîand he was the child of my only child, of Olga whom you left to die in shame. And he was a fine and a lovely man, as lovely as the morning with his strange, light eyes, and you left him at a time when his heart was broken because of this intolerable world! Oh, I know very well why you did these things, you did them to break Teresa Boselli. ‚ÄòShe will not serve me any more,‚Äô you said; ‚Äòvery well, I will show her that it pays best to serve me; I will take all things from her and leave her alone, now that she must suffer old age.‚Äô But she will not serve you; she has come here to tell you yet again that she will not serve you! He was lovely as the morning, the little Gian-Luca‚ÅÝ‚Äî‚Äù

She stopped abruptly, staring up at the Madonna, who looked down with a deprecating smile‚ÅÝ‚Äîshe could not help that deprecating smile, it was molded into the plaster.

‚ÄúAnswer me!‚Äù commanded Teresa sternly. ‚ÄúWhat have you done with Gian-Luca?‚Äù

But the Virgin was silent, and her lips were composed and gentle, like the lips of the body in the stable; for not in poor, faltering human speech could the Mother of God reply to Teresa.
